Audit Skips:

/home/mgalgs/aur-sleuth-data/bulk-reports/qwen-qwen3-235b-a22b-2507/aur-sleuth-a1x6sct4/PKGBUILD: [SKIPPED] Skipping binary file: PKGBUILD

data.reasoning: Both models skipped the only file they attempted to review (PKGBUILD), classifying it as a 'binary file' and aborting the audit. No files were actually reviewed by either model. This is a tooling/file-detection bug, not a genuine safety determination. The PKGBUILD almost certainly is a normal text file that was misidentified as binary (likely due to encoding detection heuristics flagging non-ASCII content or a false binary-detection trigger). Since zero content was analyzed, no safety verdict can be reached.
data.disagreement_analysis: There is no disagreement — both models reached identical outcomes (skipped) for the identical reason (PKGBUILD misdetected as binary). The common failure points to a shared upstream tooling issue in the binary-file detection logic, not model-specific reasoning.
coverage_issues: PKGBUILD was never reviewed — it was incorrectly flagged as a binary file and skipped, aborting the entire audit before any analysis.
No other package files (e.g., .install hooks, .SRCINFO, auxiliary scripts) were enumerated or reviewed because the initial PKGBUILD gate failed.
false_positives: The 'binary file' classification of PKGBUILD is almost certainly a false positive from the file-type detection heuristic; AUR PKGBUILDs are shell scripts (text), not binaries.
false_negatives: Unable to assess — since the PKGBUILD was never read, any genuine risks (source URLs, build/package functions, install hooks pulling nightly binaries) are entirely unevaluated. A 'ruffle-demo-nightly' package by nature fetches nightly build artifacts, which warrants scrutiny of download sources and checksum handling.
re_audit_focus: PKGBUILD — force text-mode reading and analyze source/download URLs, sha sums, build()/package()/prepare() functions
Verify nightly artifact download source legitimacy and integrity (skip-checksum patterns common in -nightly packages)
learnings: The binary-file detection heuristic is over-aggressive and misclassifies text PKGBUILDs; PKGBUILD should be whitelisted as a text file or detection should fall back to attempting UTF-8/Latin-1 decode before declaring binary.

When the initial PKGBUILD gate fails due to a skip (not a security finding), the result should be clearly distinguished as a 'tooling error / needs manual review' rather than implying any safety assessment.
A failed initial PKGBUILD read should not silently abort enumeration of the rest of the package; at minimum the tool should list remaining files so a human knows what is unreviewed.

Identical skip outcomes across multiple independent models is a strong signal of a deterministic tooling bug rather than a content-driven decision — such cases should auto-flag for re-audit with corrected file handling.
